draft.introduction: Satire 10, known as “The Vanity of Human Wishes,” surveys the futility of common prayers—for wealth, power, eloquence, military glory, long life, and beauty. Juvenal concludes that if you must pray, pray only for a sound mind in a sound body.

translation.text: Is there nothing, then, for which people should pray? If you want my counsel, you will allow the gods themselves to determine what is fitting for us and what is useful for our state. Instead of pleasant things, the gods will give us what is best. You should pray for a sound mind in a sound body. Ask for a brave soul that does not fear death, that counts the closing years of life among nature’s gifts, that can endure any kind of labor.
translation.original_text: Orandum est ut sit mens sana in corpore sano. Fortem posce animum mortis terrore carentem.

translator_notes.note: Mens sana in corpore sano (“a sound mind in a sound body”) is now a universal proverb, though Juvenal’s context is often forgotten. His point is that wisdom and physical health are the only things worth asking the gods for; all other prayers are foolish or dangerous.
